current_fact.fact_text: In 1978, Afghan intellectual Mir Akbar Khyber was assassinated in Kabul, a killing that ignited protests and unrest. Within days, his death helped trigger the Saur Revolution, a coup that toppled the government and pulled Afghanistan into decades of upheaval.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

current_fact.match_reason: Khyber’s assassination leading to revolution parallels Residente’s politically charged narratives—both show how one voice, silenced or amplified, can shift a country’s trajectory.

queue.fact_text: On April 17, 1992, the oil tanker Katina P was deliberately run aground off Maputo, Mozambique, spilling around 60,000 tons of crude oil into the Indian Ocean. The disaster blackened beaches, smothered marine life and highlighted how fragile coastal ecosystems are to human error and carelessness.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: An oil spill threatening coastal and marine life contrasts with the episode’s reverent encounter with whales, together underscoring both the beauty and vulnerability of ocean ecosystems.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 2021, the funeral of Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh, was held in the intimate setting of St George’s Chapel at Windsor Castle. Pandemic restrictions meant only 30 guests attended, turning what might have been a grand royal ceremony into a strangely pared‑back moment of public and private mourning.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A restrained royal funeral shaped by circumstance suits an episode about looking back on a difficult chapter and finding quiet resilience in the process of saying goodbye.

queue.fact_text: On April 17, 1970, as Apollo 13 was returning home, Earth itself played a starring role: the crew’s improvised slingshot around the Moon depended on our planet’s precise gravity and position. For a brief moment, three astronauts trusted a thin shell of metal and the invisible mathematics of celestial mechanics to bring them safely back to the blue sphere below.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

queue.match_reason: A story that zooms out to see Earth as a delicate navigational anchor pairs with an episode that treats one city as a microcosm of the planet’s energy, both celebrating our shared home from different scales.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1941, as Yugoslavia fell, the landscapes that would later become national parks and tourist trails were already being carved by deeper forces—tectonic plates, rivers, and time. The very mountains that shaped the war’s outcome were the result of millions of years of Earth’s slow, patient geology.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: This reframing of wartime landscapes as products of deep time echoes the episode’s invitation to see our world as a 'slow planet,' with music that moves at geological—not political—speed.

queue.fact_text: On April 17, 1941, as Yugoslavia surrendered, countless animals and forests across the Balkans remained indifferent to human borders and battles. Wolves, bears and beech forests continued their cycles, reminding us that nature’s timelines stretch far beyond any armistice.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: The contrast between human conflict and nature’s continuity pairs with an episode centered on wild marine life, both highlighting worlds that carry on beyond our wars.

queue.fact_text: On April 17, 1970, as Apollo 13’s capsule drifted through the dark, crew members caught fleeting glimpses of the stars and the thin blue halo of Earth’s atmosphere. Those views, framed by danger and silence, reinforced just how small and fragile our home appears from space.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A drifting spacecraft framed by stars and the glow of Earth matches the open, rule‑free nocturnal journey promised by the first Evening Flow—both quiet invitations to float.

published.fact_text: In 2014, astronomers announced Kepler-186f, the first confirmed Earth-sized planet orbiting within the habitable zone of another star. This distant world, 500 light-years away, circles a dim red dwarf and may have liquid water on its surface, radically expanding our sense of where life might exist in the galaxy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A new Earth-like world embodies the idea of 'space' as possibility, mirroring the episode’s theme of reframing emptiness as creative potential and its cosmic-tinged title.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 1970, Apollo 13—crippled by an oxygen tank explosion 200,000 miles from home—splashed down safely in the Pacific Ocean. What could have been NASA's darkest tragedy became a legendary story of calm improvisation, human resilience and precise engineering under pressure.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: Apollo 13’s narrow escape from disaster resonates with the episode’s theme that storms eventually pass, pairing a near-tragedy turned triumph with reflective, hopeful rock.

published.fact_text: In 1998, Space Shuttle Columbia launched STS-90, the final Spacelab mission, turning the orbiter into a temporary flying neuroscience laboratory. For 16 days, astronauts and rats alike floated in microgravity so scientists could study how brains and nervous systems adapt when up and down disappear.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: A brain-focused mission drifting in orbit fits with the cinematic, drifting textures of trip-hop and the episode’s exploration of boundary-blurring, experimental soundscapes.

published.fact_text: In 1492, the Capitulations of Santa Fe formalized an audacious deal: Christopher Columbus would sail west into the unknown, and in return be granted lofty titles and a share of whatever he found. Signed in a military camp outside Granada, the document helped launch Europe’s age of oceanic exploration—and centuries of global upheaval.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A contract that launched transoceanic voyages aligns with an episode about travel and shifting perspective, both mapping inner journeys onto literal expeditions.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1524, Florentine explorer Giovanni da Verrazzano became the first recorded European to enter what is now New York Harbor. He sailed past the future site of the Statue of Liberty, unaware he was sketching the earliest European descriptions of one of Earth’s most iconic urban coastlines.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: Verrazzano’s sea voyage from the Mediterranean to unknown shores pairs naturally with a boat-themed episode that sails sonically across Mediterranean coasts.

published.fact_text: In 1964, Jerrie Mock quietly became the first woman to fly solo around the world, piloting a single‑engine Cessna named Spirit of Columbus. Over 29 and a half days, this Ohio mother of three navigated storms, bureaucracies and sleepless nights, proving that long-distance aviation was not just a man’s domain.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: A solo flight circling the planet fits perfectly with an episode designed for movement and travel, capturing the forward momentum and fresh-start energy of Mock’s journey.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 1942, French General Henri Giraud executed a daring escape from the supposedly inescapable Königstein Fortress, a cliff‑top prison in Saxony. Using a smuggled rope and careful observation, the 62‑year‑old officer rappelled down a 40‑meter wall and slipped through enemy territory to freedom.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: Giraud’s audacious break for freedom echoes the rebellious, rule‑breaking spirit of rock celebrated in this episode, where loud guitars stand in for daring escapes.

published.fact_text: In 1783, near the end of the American Revolutionary War, a little‑known skirmish called Colbert’s Raid unfolded at Arkansas Post. Spanish forces under Captain Jacobo du Breuil repelled British irregulars along the Mississippi frontier, a reminder that the war’s outcome was shaped not just in famous cities, but in remote river outposts too.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A small, riverine battle on a quiet frontier fits the episode’s focus on overlooked, slower spaces that still shape history, with flowing jazz mirroring the Mississippi currents.

published.fact_text: In 1797, citizens of Verona rose up against French occupation in an eight‑day revolt known as the Veronese Easter. Church bells were rung as covert signals, and ordinary townspeople briefly turned their Renaissance city into a maze of barricades and ambushes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: An Italian city in revolt embodies both ‘fog’ and ‘fire’—everyday life disrupted by sudden passion—matching an episode that explores contrasting Italian moods and songwriters.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1863, Union cavalry under Colonel Benjamin Grierson launched Grierson’s Raid, a daring 600‑mile ride through Confederate Mississippi. The small force tied up far larger enemy armies and helped pave the way for the Union victory at Vicksburg by attacking from deep in the South’s own backyard.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A fast, disruptive cavalry raid through enemy territory matches the episode’s high-voltage rock energy and highway imagery—music for charging straight through obstacles.

published.fact_text: In 1895, the Treaty of Shimonoseki ended the First Sino‑Japanese War, forcing the Qing dynasty to cede Taiwan, the Penghu Islands, and influence over Korea to Japan. The agreement dramatically reshaped power in East Asia and foreshadowed the region’s turbulent 20th century.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

published.match_reason: Both the treaty and the episode trace how power and culture shift across borders over time—one through geopolitical terms, the other through the evolution of a global genre born at the periphery.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 1912, Imperial Russian troops opened fire on striking goldfield workers along the Lena River in Siberia, killing at least 150 people in what became known as the Lena massacre. The brutality shocked Russian society and fueled revolutionary anger that would later topple the empire.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A violent crackdown on workers’ rights directly reflects the themes of resistance, injustice and political anger central to Rage Against the Machine’s story and sound.

published.fact_text: In 1941, the Axis invasion of Yugoslavia concluded when the country signed an armistice with Germany and Italy, just 11 days after the attack began. The swift collapse set the stage for one of World War II’s most complex resistance movements, with partisans, royalists, and occupiers battling across rugged mountains and deep forests.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: The mountainous battlegrounds of Yugoslavia echo the episode’s focus on music and memory in alpine landscapes, connecting rugged terrains with layered human stories.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 1944, fighters from the Communist‑led Greek People’s Liberation Army attacked the smaller National and Social Liberation resistance group. The clash ended with the surrender of the latter and the murder of its leader, Dimitrios Psarros, revealing bitter fractures within the anti‑Axis resistance.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: An internal struggle within a resistance movement resonates with Tuareg music born from exile, division and survival, both telling stories of liberation that are far from simple.

published.fact_text: In 1349, the Bavand dynasty’s rule over Mazandaran in northern Iran abruptly ended with the murder of its last ruler, Hasan II. This assassination closed a dynasty that had survived for nearly seven centuries in the shadow of much larger empires.

episode.title: Morning Drops #18 - Sardegna
episode.description: Yasmin traces her connection to Sardegna — the island where her Italian journey began. Through music and memory, she shares the story of how this place shaped her family's path, and why it still holds such a deep place in her heart.
songs: Mahmood & Tenores di Bitti - Com’è profondo il mare

published.match_reason: The fall of a long‑lived regional dynasty pairs with an episode about deep attachment to a specific land, both exploring how local identity endures beyond shifting powers.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1971, amid a brutal war of independence, the Provisional Government of Bangladesh was formed. Operating in exile, it became the political backbone of a future nation that would emerge from one of the 20th century’s most violent birth struggles.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: The painstaking formation of a new government under fire reflects long, messy processes of growth; the episode’s focus on persistence and learning mirrors a nation learning to become itself.

published.fact_text: On April 17, 1975, Khmer Rouge forces captured Phnom Penh, ending the Cambodian Civil War and beginning the Cambodian genocide. Within hours the victors began forcing city dwellers into the countryside, emptying an entire capital in one of history’s most chilling social experiments.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A regime that forcibly severed people from their homes and connections is a dark mirror to the episode’s reflection on connection and disconnection, prompting deeper questions about control and freedom.

published.fact_text: In 1978, Afghan intellectual Mir Akbar Khyber was assassinated in Kabul, a killing that ignited protests and unrest. Within days, his death helped trigger the Saur Revolution, a coup that toppled the government and pulled Afghanistan into decades of upheaval.
